text:
They drifted over algae farms and deepwater fish schools, then, as they descended, came in among the gardens where the heat of a lava stream let them cultivate rarer plants. Many glowed on their own, a wondrous blend of blue, green, yellow, and violet light.

The Merfolk who worked here treated the gardens as an art, and their mere existence delighted everyone. As the hunting Shoal passed, the gardeners bowed their heads in respect. Children shadowed them for a long while, and more than one hand got slapped when they tried to touch the humans who looked so strange to them. Beards captivated them most of all. And since the hunt had been set for an hour when they were still allowed outside, they were not about to miss the chance to gawk at the hairy, ungainly sailors from up close.

No mermaid knew exactly what the dome was made of, the one that arched over the city of Geynea as a half-sphere to keep the ocean out and hold a bubble of breathable air. For lack of a better word they called it the Membrane. It was thin, sometimes brittle as glass to the eye, sometimes elastic as hide. Translucent, iridescing with all the colors of the rainbow, and seeming to glow with a dull white light. The priestesses said it was a peculiar alloy of sorcery and refined technology, its exact nature lost with the rest of the Sirens’ knowledge. The Sirens had helped the Merfolk build their cities, taught them how to tend the marvels that sustained their way of life afterward, but never told them how to make such things themselves, or how to improve them. It all came from an age the Sirens wanted to forget, so they could return to a pure and blessed way of living. For millennia nothing had ever faltered or failed, and the Merfolk had accepted that the ancients could build for eternity. They did not press the Sirens further.

The Hunters had their own entrance to the city, so they could move the offerings quickly without disturbing daily life. The Shoal gathered at the gate and, all at once, began to sing a single set pitch. Learning that exact note and holding it clean was a Hunter’s first lesson. Without it they would warp the harmony and risk the gate’s Membrane staying solid and impassable. At the sound, the surface of the gate began to ripple like water. The Shoal drew down to the very bottom, to the level of the floor beyond, and slid through.

Melusine went from water into open air. On the polished stone she shoved the three sailors she had dragged this far ahead of her, then rolled aside to make room for the sisters behind. She lay on her back and waited while one of the Merfolk posted here ran off to alert the others that they had returned.

Only when busy hands seized the offerings to carry them to their assigned quarters, and when the wounded sailors were tended just enough to keep them alive a while longer, did she begin the change. It had never come easy to her. First her claws retracted, as if nails were being driven into her fingertips. Then the tiny, near-invisible scales sloughed from her skin, leaving it soft and vulnerable. Last came the worst of it, when her mermaid’s tail became legs. Every time it felt like being flayed as the thickened turquoise fish-skin and pearly scales peeled away, and when the two legs split it was as if her limbs were being torn from her.

She did not even feel when her sharp teeth withdrew to make room for her ordinary human ones. The slits of her gills sealed, and her diaphragm began to move again, drawing stabbing air into her lungs. Her whole body burned with pain, and she was still gasping on the floor even after those few minutes that had felt like an eternity. It helped a little that the others did not bear it any better.

A teenage girl from the servant caste stood over her in a brown tunic and trousers, holding out a light robe. Melusine pushed herself up from the floor, thanked her, swung the robe over her shoulders, and shook out her still-wet, wavy lilac hair. Then she looked over the Shoal and began to count.

Forty-six.

Fifty had set out a week ago, if her sense of time was right. She had not brought home four sisters. It had been far worse before, and there had been times they were lucky enough to return whole. She knew all their names, so it took only seconds to tally the ones whose faces she did not see.

They did not bring their dead home. The sea would take their bodies either way, and it mattered more to bring down as many humans as they could. Sometimes, if they were fortunate, the crews who scoured and salvaged wrecks found a sister’s remains. Even then they brought back only a memento: a lock of hair, a bone, a larger scale for the family. The body was laid to rest where it lay.

That was no longer Melusine’s task.
